title: Module 2 quiz: Connecting the assistant to the real world

questions.prompt: What's the right OAuth scope strategy for an assistant's v1?

choices.text: Least-privilege + incremental: smallest scope that does the job; add broader scopes only when users try features that need them. Each ask is tied to a specific value moment. Major providers (Google, Microsoft, Salesforce) support this pattern.

questions.explanation: Narrow + incremental scoping is the canonical pattern. Reduces consent-screen drop-off, limits blast radius on incidents, builds user trust through ramped permission asks.

questions.prompt: Why is MCP especially valuable for assistant products?

choices.text: It's mandatory for major LLM providers.

Assistants need many tools, last years, ship on multiple surfaces, and share integrations across products. MCP turns integrations from per-product code into shared infrastructure — build once, reuse across many assistants. The reuse compounds.
It's faster than direct API calls.

questions.explanation: MCP value scales with assistant lineup. Build once; reuse across products. Combine community servers (filesystem, GitHub, Slack) with your custom MCP servers for proprietary tools.

questions.prompt: User clicks 'disconnect Google.' Your code deletes tokens from your DB. Next reconnect skips the consent screen — they get the same scopes silently. Why?

choices.text: You deleted locally but didn't revoke at the provider. Google still has the app authorized. Clean disconnect = revoke at provider via their API + delete from your DB + halt dependent background tasks + surface the impact to the user.

questions.explanation: Half-disconnects break trust. Always revoke at the provider on user disconnect. Then delete locally, halt schedules, surface clearly.

questions.prompt: How should OAuth tokens be stored in your assistant's backend?

choices.text: Encrypted at rest with strong keys, strictly scoped per user, never logged, with audit access controls. Treat tokens like passwords — their blast radius if leaked is the assistant's full OAuth scope on the user's account.

questions.explanation: Tokens are password-equivalent. Encrypt, scope per user, never log. Rotate refresh tokens; revoke on disconnect; audit access.

questions.prompt: Your v1 design has 28 tools. Pre-launch trimming: what's the heuristic?

choices.text: For each tool: does v1 JTBD genuinely need it? If not, cut. Default v1 to read-heavy / write-light / no-destructive. Each tool is risk surface — eval needs, failure modes, prompt cost, approval decisions. Earn the surface area as trust grows.

questions.explanation: v1 surface area should be ruthlessly cut. Read-heavy gives value at low risk; writes come with trust; destructive tools wait for proven patterns. 'While we're at it' tools balloon surface area silently.
